message.content:
POLARIS context: compose renders 346 baskets via OpenRouter (compose model = z-ai/glm-5.2, plus an
NLI verifier judge that shares the SAME OpenRouter account). Repo: /workspace/POLARIS (readable),
compose worktree: /home/polaris/wt/compose (writable).

THE DISPUTE YOU ARE SETTLING:
- Claim A (original Fable, baked into OVERNIGHT_MASTER_BRIEF.md:9): "compose is already ~48-way
  parallel; the real ceiling is the OpenRouter 429 rate limit."
- Claim B (later Fable gate, commit 0615bc5, backed by an A/B measurement): the ceiling was a
  missing await — _draft_passes_wrapper (generator/abstractive_writer.py:661) is a sync def that was
  called with NO await from the async coroutine _pre_pass_one_basket (:749), blocking the event loop
  on every NLI POST. True verify concurrency was 1. Measured 2.06s serialized -> 0.60s parallel.
  Under Claim B, the 429 could NOT have been binding pre-fix (you cannot rate-limit 1 in-flight req).

SECURITY: the OpenRouter API key is a secret. NEVER print it, never echo it, never paste it into
your result. Refer to it only as $OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Redact it if it appears in any output.

HONESTY: report REAL measured numbers. If you cannot measure something, say so. Do not estimate a
rate limit and present it as measured.


YOU ARE FABLE, THE INDEPENDENT GATE. Settle the dispute with the evidence below. You did not write
any of this code and you have no stake in either claim being right.

RECON (what the account is entitled to + what the logs really show):
{
  "key_found": true,
  "key_location": "Variable name: OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Configured in the file /workspace/POLARIS/.env (single line `OPENROUTER_API_KEY=...`; the template is /workspace/POLARIS/.env.example:15). It is NOT in the shell env by default and there is no .env in /home/polaris/wt/compose. Every run loads it by sourcing that file: /home/polaris/wt/compose/launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh:12 does `set -a; . /workspace/POLARIS/.env 2>/dev/null; set +a`. Code reads it via os.environ at /workspace/POLARIS/src/polaris_graph/llm/openrouter_client.py:58 and roles/openrouter_role_transport.py:1228. Value never printed.",
  "account_facts": {
    "credits_remaining": "MEASURED via GET https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/credits: total_credits=2550, total_usage=2329.168087508 -> ~220.83 credits remaining. The /key endpoint reports limit=null and limit_remaining=null, i.e. this key has NO per-key credit cap; usage=2272.36 (key-scoped), usage_daily=88.28, usage_weekly=282.54, usage_monthly=368.20.",
    "is_free_tier": false,
    "rate_limit_interval": "10s -- but OpenRouter itself marks this field deprecated ('This field is deprecated and safe to ignore.'), so it is NOT a real published limit.",
    "rate_limit_requests": "-1 (i.e. no numeric request cap returned; the field is flagged deprecated). OpenRouter does NOT expose a concrete requests-per-interval number for this key. Any specific RPS/RPM ceiling anyone quotes for this account is NOT measured and is not obtainable from the API.",
    "raw_response": "{\"data\":{\"label\":\"sk-or-v1-[REDACTED]\",\"is_management_key\":false,\"is_provisioning_key\":false,\"limit\":null,\"limit_reset\":null,\"limit_remaining\":null,\"include_byok_in_limit\":false,\"usage\":2272.360166297,\"usage_daily\":88.283711174,\"usage_weekly\":282.540299187,\"usage_monthly\":368.201526559,\"byok_usage\":0,\"byok_usage_daily\":0,\"byok_usage_weekly\":0,\"byok_usage_monthly\":0,\"is_free_tier\":false,\"expires_at\":null,\"creator_user_id\":\"user_39Ya2npurVBXLBcvPUDnOfIlX5R\",\"rate_limit\":{\"requests\":-1,\"interval\":\"10s\",\"note\":\"This field is deprecated and safe to ignore.\"}}}  [key label redacted]"
  },
  "evidence_of_429": {
    "count": "17 real HTTP 429s TOTAL across EVERY log file on disk. Breakdown: 12 in the currently-LIVE run /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log (window 08:33:12 -> 14:51:34 = 6h18m, containing 3656 OpenRouter POSTs -> 429 rate = 0.33% of calls, ~1.9 per hour); 4 in /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_sonnet_hands/compose.log (2h10m, 1183 POSTs); 1 in /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter3/compose.log (1h14m, 703 POSTs). ZERO 429s in s5_gear_iter1 (396 POSTs), s5_gear_iter1_pinned (794 POSTs), s5_gear_round_iter1 (23 POSTs), POLARIS/outputs/s5_gear/compose_iter2.log (145 POSTs). ATTRIBUTION: all 17 were logged by src.polaris_graph.llm.entailment_judge (the NLI verifier). ZERO 429s from the compose writer model (openrouter_client / z-ai glm-5.2). SEVERITY: every single one was logged as 'attempt 1/6' -- i.e. all recovered on the first retry; no 429 ever escalated past attempt 1 and none exhausted the retry budget. This is a sporadic 429, NOT a binding ceiling: it is nowhere near 'every other call'.",
    "logs_searched": "/workspace/POLARIS/logs (pg_cost_ledger.jsonl, session_log.md, bug_log.md), /workspace/POLARIS/outputs (incl. s5_gear/compose_iter2.log, fetch_corpus_replay_*.log), /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/* (s5_gear_iter1, s5_gear_iter1_pinned, s5_gear_round_iter1, s5_gear_iter2, s5_gear_iter3, s5_gear_iter4 [LIVE], s5_sonnet_hands), /home/polaris/wt/compose/logs. Search terms: 'HTTP/1.1 429', '429', 'rate limit'/'rate-limit'/'ratelimit', 'Retry-After'. Note: a naive grep for '429' over-counts badly (2/3 of hits are token counts like '429 reasoning tokens' / '1429 chunks'); only 'HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests' lines are real.",
    "retry_after_values": "NO Retry-After header was ever observed. grep -ci 'retry-after' over every compose log = 0 hits. The only backoff value that appears is 'rate-limit backoff 15.0s', and it appears 19 times with NO other value ever seen. That 15.0s is CLIENT-SIDE, not the server: entailment_judge.py:216 _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S = 15.0, and _rate_limit_backoff_s() (entailment_judge.py:486-509) returns exactly the floor when the exponential term <= floor and returns a JITTERED (non-round) value otherwise. A server Retry-After would be honored verbatim (line 503) -- the fact that every observed value is exactly 15.0 confirms OpenRouter returned no Retry-After. So: we have ZERO measured server-supplied rate-limit guidance. The '15s' figure is our own constant, not OpenRouter's."
  },
  "configured_concurrency": {
    "PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY": "24 -- SET explicitly at /home/polaris/wt/compose/launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh:29 (the launcher of the currently-running compose, pid 966951, `bash launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh` started 08:33). Code default would be 8 (abstractive_writer.py _DEFAULT_CONCURRENCY). Other launchers in the worktree set it to 3 (iter1/gear scripts), 24 (iter3/iter4), or 12 (iter5). NOT set in /workspace/POLARIS/.env.",
    "PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM": "30 -- SET explicitly at /home/polaris/wt/compose/launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh:30. Code default is 5 (/workspace/compose_wt/src/providers/llm_provider.py:79 `_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM = int(os.getenv(\"PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM\", \"5\"))`). This is the GLOBAL semaphore every LLM call acquires. NOT set in /workspace/POLARIS/.env.",
    "PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY": "NOT SET ANYWHERE -> falls back to the code default 4 (/home/polaris/wt/compose/src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:56 DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4). It is absent from /workspace/POLARIS/.env and absent from launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh (only prose in .codex/ design docs mentions a value of 2). THIS IS THE LOAD-BEARING FINDING: the NLI verifier's provider POSTs are capped by a PROCESS-GLOBAL BoundedSemaphore of 4 (acquire_judge_slot), so verify-side OpenRouter concurrency can never exceed 4 regardless of the writer's 24 or the global 30 -- and 4 concurrent NLI POSTs is not a plausible way to hit an account rate limit.",
    "_caveat": "The live process (pid 966951) runs as root, so /proc/966951/environ is unreadable to me (NO_SUDO). These values are read from the launcher script that is provably its parent (root pid 817298 `bash launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh`, and the pid 966951 cmdline `python -u scripts/run_s5_i3.py --out /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/...` matches that script's OUT path exactly). I could not read the env block directly.",
    "_note_on_48_way": "The '~48-way parallel' figure in OVERNIGHT_MASTER_BRIEF.md:9 reconciles as PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=24 x PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 (launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh:37). That is WRITER-side fan-out only. The verifier/NLI side is separately clamped to 4."
  }
}

PROBE (what we actually measured):
{
  "probed": true,
  "method": "Standalone probe at /tmp/or_probe.py (NOT in a worktree). Real POSTs to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions, model z-ai/glm-5.2 (the compose model — and, verified, ALSO the NLI judge's model: entailment_judge.py:106 _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_MODEL = \"z-ai/glm-5.2\", so the probe exercises the exact path every historical 429 came from). Prompt \"Say OK.\", max_tokens=8, temperature=0. Key loaded by sourcing /workspace/POLARIS/.env into env; never printed, never logged (probe explicitly .replace(KEY,\"[REDACTED]\") on any error body). Closed-loop bursts at a fixed asyncio.Semaphore concurrency, n = 2x concurrency per level. Ramp 1/2/4/8/16/32, then escalated 48/64/96/128 when nothing broke. Every response header key was dumped verbatim rather than inferred. 1184 real requests total (128 + 672 + 384 across three runs). Total spend measured from GET /api/v1/credits before/after: total_usage 2329.168 -> 2329.679 = ~0.51 credits.",
  "measured": {
    "headers_seen": "THERE ARE NO RATE-LIMIT HEADERS. This is a measured negative, not a failure to look. The COMPLETE verbatim set of response header keys OpenRouter returns on a 200 from this account is: ['access-control-allow-origin', 'access-control-expose-headers', 'cf-ray', 'connection', 'content-encoding', 'content-type', 'date', 'permissions-policy', 'referrer-policy', 'server', 'transfer-encoding', 'x-content-type-options', 'x-generation-id']. NO X-RateLimit-Limit. NO X-RateLimit-Remaining. NO X-RateLimit-Reset. NO Retry-After. NO ratelimit-* of any spelling. The probe filtered on ('x-ratelimit','retry-after','ratelimit','x-request-id','x-openrouter') across all 1184 responses and matched ZERO headers. So the single most direct question -- 'what does the server say the rate limit is' -- has a definitive answer: the server says NOTHING. There is no published, header-exposed rate limit to read, which is consistent with recon's finding that /api/v1/key returns requests=-1 on a field OpenRouter itself flags deprecated.",
    "first_429_at": "NEVER. Zero 429s in 1184 real requests, at concurrency 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 48, 64, 96 and 128, at a sustained measured 17.62 req/s. FIRST_429_AT_CONCURRENCY=None. I could not induce a 429 at 128-way concurrency, which is 5.3x the writer's configured PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=24, 4.3x the global PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=30, 2.7x the '~48-way' figure in the brief, and 32x the verifier's actual PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY semaphore of 4. I did not push past 128 because the goal was to find the ceiling, and a ceiling that is not reached at 32x the real verify concurrency is already conclusively non-binding.",
    "max_sustained_concurrency": "128 concurrent in-flight requests, 256 requests, 100% success (256 ok / 0 429 / 0 other). Never found a concurrency ceiling. The only failure seen anywhere was a single client-side httpx ConnectTimeout during the 48/64/96/128 sweep -- a LOCAL socket/connection-pool artifact, not a server rejection, and notably not a 429.",
    "throughput_req_per_s": "Measured (requests / wall-clock per burst): conc=1 -> 0.66 req/s | conc=2 -> 1.25 | conc=4 -> 1.55 | conc=8 -> 1.42 | conc=16 -> 2.72 | conc=32 -> 13.21 | conc=64 -> 11.06 | conc=128 -> 17.62 req/s. Peak sustained = 17.62 req/s with a 100% success rate. NOTE the shape: throughput does NOT scale linearly with concurrency (32-way already gives 13.2 req/s; quadrupling to 128-way buys only 17.6). That flattening is upstream provider queueing -- it shows up as LATENCY, not as 429. There IS a soft ceiling out there, but it is a saturation/queueing ceiling, not a rate-limiter ceiling, and it sits far above anything compose is configured to generate.",
    "latency_p50_p95": "Per-level, successful requests only: conc=1 p50=1.50s p95=2.07s | conc=2 p50=1.36s p95=1.66s | conc=4 p50=1.26s p95=3.25s | conc=8 p50=1.60s p95=9.34s | conc=16 p50=1.61s p95=10.89s | conc=32 p50=1.51s p95=3.22s | conc=64 p50=1.84s p95=3.09s | conc=128 p50=1.60s p95=6.49s. p50 is remarkably FLAT (~1.3-1.8s) from 1-way all the way to 128-way -- the server is not slowing individual requests down as concurrency climbs. All the pain is in the tail (p95 blows out to 6-11s), i.e. queueing, not throttling."
  },
  "contention_note": "YES -- CONTENDED, and I am flagging it loudly rather than dressing the numbers up as clean. `ps -p 966951` confirmed the root-owned `python -u scripts/run_s5_i3.py ... --out /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/...` was RUNNING before the probe (ELAPSED 01:44:27) and STILL RUNNING after it, i.e. it was hammering the SAME OpenRouter account and the SAME model (z-ai/glm-5.2) for the entire probe window. So every throughput/latency number above is the account's capacity MINUS whatever that run was consuming -- they are a LOWER BOUND on true headroom, not a clean measurement. Two things follow, and note they point in OPPOSITE directions, so be careful which way you lean:  (1) The THROUGHPUT and LATENCY numbers are DIRTY and pessimistic. 17.62 req/s and those p95 tails are what was left over while a 24x2-way compose run was already in flight. Do not quote 17.62 req/s as 'the account's max throughput' -- the uncontended number is higher by an unknown margin. I cannot clean this up without killing a live root-owned production run, which I will not do.  (2) The ZERO-429 result is *strengthened*, not weakened, by the contention -- and this is the load-bearing point. I stacked 128 concurrent requests ON TOP OF an already-running production compose and STILL could not provoke a single 429. A rate limit that cannot be tripped by (live run + 128 extra concurrent) is not a limit that was binding at (live run alone). The contention makes the negative result more conservative, not less.  BOTTOM LINE ON THE DISPUTE: this probe FALSIFIES Claim A. The brief's assertion (OVERNIGHT_MASTER_BRIEF.md:9) that 'the real ceiling is the OpenRouter 429 rate limit' is not supported by any measurement I can produce: no rate-limit headers exist to read, and no 429 can be induced at 32x the verifier's real concurrency even under contention. It is consistent with Claim B. Two independent facts corroborate B: (a) recon found all 17 historical 429s were logged by entailment_judge and ZERO by the writer, and every one recovered on 'attempt 1/6' -- sporadic upstream noise, not a ceiling; (b) the verify side is clamped by a process-global BoundedSemaphore of 4 (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset -> DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4), and pre-fix the missing await made TRUE in-flight verify concurrency 1. You cannot rate-limit a single in-flight request. The 429 was never the ceiling; the blocking sync call was.",
  "raw_output": "$ ps -p 966951  ->  966951 root 01:44:27 python -u scripts/run_s5_i3.py --out /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/... [ALIVE THROUGHOUT PROBE]\n\n=== RUN 1: ramp 1..32 (/tmp/or_probe_out.txt) ===\n[header recon] status=200 keys=['access-control-allow-origin', 'access-control-expose-headers', 'cf-ray', 'connection', 'content-encoding', 'content-type', 'date', 'permissions-policy', 'referrer-policy', 'server', 'transfer-encoding', 'x-content-type-options', 'x-generation-id']\nMODEL=z-ai/glm-5.2  ts=14:54:03\n[conc= 1] n=  4 wall=  6.10s  ok=  4 429=  0 other= 0  thr= 0.66 req/s  ok_thr= 0.66  p50= 1.50s p95= 2.07s\n[conc= 2] n=  4 wall=  3.19s  ok=  4 429=  0 other= 0  thr= 1.25 req/s  ok_thr= 1.25  p50= 1.36s p95= 1.66s\n[conc= 4] n=  8 wall=  5.15s  ok=  8 429=  0 other= 0  thr= 1.55 req/s  ok_thr= 1.55  p50= 1.26s p95= 3.25s\n[conc= 8] n= 16 wall= 11.31s  ok= 16 429=  0 other= 0  thr= 1.42 req/s  ok_thr= 1.42  p50= 1.60s p95= 9.34s\n[conc=16] n= 32 wall= 11.76s  ok= 32 429=  0 other= 0  thr= 2.72 req/s  ok_thr= 2.72  p50= 1.61s p95=10.89s\n[conc=32] n= 64 wall=  4.85s  ok= 64 429=  0 other= 0  thr=13.21 req/s  ok_thr=13.21  p50= 1.51s p95= 3.22s\n\n=== VERBATIM RATE-LIMIT-ISH RESPONSE HEADERS (samples) ===\nNONE. OpenRouter returned NO x-ratelimit-* / retry-after headers on any response.\n\nTOTAL REQUESTS=128  TOTAL_429=0\nFIRST_429_AT_CONCURRENCY=None\n\n=== RUN 2: escalation 48/64/96/128 (/tmp/or_probe_high.txt) ===\nMODEL=z-ai/glm-5.2  ts=14:55:00\n    NON-200/429 status=-1: EXC ConnectTimeout:        <-- client-side local socket timeout, NOT a server rejection\nNONE. OpenRouter returned NO x-ratelimit-* / retry-after headers on any response.\nTOTAL REQUESTS=672  TOTAL_429=0\nFIRST_429_AT_CONCURRENCY=None\n\n=== RUN 3: high levels re-run with per-level detail preserved (/tmp/or_probe_high2.txt) ===\n[conc=64]  n=128 wall= 11.57s  ok=128 429=  0 other= 0  thr=11.06 req/s  ok_thr=11.06  p50= 1.84s p95= 3.09s\n[conc=128] n=256 wall= 14.53s  ok=256 429=  0 other= 0  thr=17.62 req/s  ok_thr=17.62  p50= 1.60s p95= 6.49s\nNONE. OpenRouter returned NO x-ratelimit-* / retry-after headers on any response.\nTOTAL REQUESTS=384  TOTAL_429=0\nFIRST_429_AT_CONCURRENCY=None\n\n=== NLI JUDGE SHARES THE PROBED MODEL (so the probe covers the 429 source path) ===\n/workspace/POLARIS/src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py:106:\n  _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_MODEL = \"z-ai/glm-5.2\"\n\n=== SPEND (GET /api/v1/credits, before -> after) ===\nbefore: total_credits 2550  total_usage 2329.168087508  -> remaining ~220.83\nafter : total_credits 2550  total_usage 2329.678976975  -> remaining  220.321\nprobe cost = ~0.51 credits for 1184 requests. Trivial.\n\n=== AGGREGATE ===\n1184 real requests | max concurrency 128 | 429s: 0 | Retry-After observed: never (no 429 to carry one) | rate-limit headers: none exist\n$ ps -p 966951 [after probe] -> STILL RUNNING (contention present for entire probe)"
}

ANSWER, precisely:
1. What is the REAL OpenRouter rate limit for this account? (number + interval, from the headers/API,
   not inferred.)
2. Post-fix, is the 429 the BINDING constraint on compose, or is it not? Be specific about the
   arithmetic: at the configured concurrency, how many req/s does compose actually generate, and how
   does that compare to the measured limit?
3. Was Claim A right, Claim B right, or were both right in sequence (A describes the wall we had not
   yet reached because B was capping us at 1)?
4. If the probe was contaminated by the root-owned job, say the evidence is INSUFFICIENT rather than
   guessing. An honest "we could not measure this cleanly, kill PID 966951 and re-run" is a better
   answer than a confident wrong one.
5. Concrete recommended settings, and an estimated wall-clock to render all 346 baskets — with the
   assumption that estimate rests on made explicit.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll do a few quick ground-truth checks before rendering the verdict: confirm the fix is present in the compose worktree, confirm the semaphore default, and pull basket-completion pacing from the live log to base the wall-clock estimate on something measured.

content.content:
# 4 mirror-chain hosts ([friendli, novita, z-ai, phala]); with burst-spread this is ~1 POST/host
# normally and a worst-case ceiling of 4 on the lead if the alternates are unreachable — comfortably
# below the ~32-simultaneous burst that 429-stormed friendli. Operators can raise this via the env if
# the provider capacity allows (the memory standard is "<= 4 agents in flight").
DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4

_LOCK = threading.Lock()
_SEMAPHORE: threading.BoundedSemaphore | None = None
_SEMAPHORE_BOUND: int | None = None

87:    malformed value -> ``DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY`` (a typo must not silently disable the de-storm).
95:        return DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY

input.binding_constraint: Client-side self-imposed concurrency caps plus upstream model latency — NOT the rate limit. Specifically: (1) the NLI verifier is clamped to 4 concurrent POSTs by the process-global BoundedSemaphore (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset -> DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4, /home/polaris/wt/compose/src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:55) — visible in the live log as verify-heavy pre-pass batches blowing past wall_cap (elapsed=2567.1s vs wall_cap=1700s, abandoned=13); (2) PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=30 clamps the writer's 24x2=48-way fan-out, so true writer concurrency is <=30 minus judge slots; (3) underneath both, z-ai/glm-5.2 generation latency (~6-12s per writer call, measured in the live log) and upstream provider queueing (probe p95 tail 6-11s — pain shows up as latency, never as 429). The eventual account-level ceiling is a saturation/queueing knee somewhere above 17.6 req/s sustained, not a 429 rate limiter.
input.reasoning: Q1 — the REAL rate limit: there is no measurable published rate limit for this account. Measured negatives, not failures to look: (a) /api/v1/key returns rate_limit requests=-1, interval=10s, on a field OpenRouter itself marks 'deprecated and safe to ignore'; limit=null (no per-key credit cap; paid tier, ~220.8 credits remaining); (b) across 1184 real probe requests the COMPLETE response header set contains NO x-ratelimit-*, no Retry-After, nothing — the server publishes no limit; (c) 0 429s at concurrency 1->128 sustained 17.62 req/s, stacked ON TOP of the live production run hammering the same account and same model (z-ai/glm-5.2, which is verifiably also the NLI judge's model, entailment_judge.py:106). Any specific RPS/RPM number anyone quotes for this account is invented. Q2 — arithmetic, post-fix: the live iter4 run generated 3708 OpenRouter POSTs in 6h26m (08:33:12->14:59:40) = 0.16 req/s average. Absolute peak burst is bounded by the semaphores: ~26 writer slots / ~6s per call ≈ 4.3 req/s plus 4 judge slots / ~1.5s p50 ≈ 2.7 req/s ≈ 7 req/s worst case — still 2.5x under the 17.62 req/s measured-under-contention LOWER BOUND at which zero 429s occurred. Average load is >100x under it. The 12 429s in the live log are 0.33% of calls (~1.9/hr), ALL from entailment_judge (zero from the writer), ALL recovered on attempt 1/6, and carried no Retry-After (every observed backoff is exactly our own client constant 15.0s, entailment_judge.py:217). That is sporadic upstream provider noise, not a binding ceiling. Q3 — A is falsified, not merely superseded: 'both in sequence' would require the 429 wall to exist somewhere ahead of us; the probe went looking for it at 128-way (2.7x the brief's own '~48-way' figure, 32x real verify concurrency) on top of a live run and it is not there. Pre-fix, B is independently confirmed: _draft_passes_wrapper is a sync def (abstractive_writer.py:661) that was called without await, blocking the loop on every NLI POST — true verify concurrency 1, and you cannot rate-limit one in-flight request at 0.16 req/s. The fix is verified present in the worktree: both call sites now do `await asyncio.to_thread(_draft_passes_wrapper, ...)` (lines ~833-835 and ~912-914), matching the measured 2.06s->0.60s A/B. Q4 — contamination: the probe WAS contended (pid 966951 alive throughout), but the contamination is asymmetric: it makes throughput/latency numbers pessimistic lower bounds while making the zero-429 result STRONGER (live run + 128 extra concurrent still could not trip the limiter). The verdict does not need a clean re-run; only the exact uncontended max-throughput number does, and nothing here depends on it.
input.headroom: On sustained rate: >100x (compose generates 0.16 req/s average vs >=17.62 req/s demonstrated with zero 429s — itself a contended lower bound with no ceiling found). On peak burst: >=2.5x even in the worst semaphore-saturated case (~7 req/s). On concurrency: no 429 at 128 in-flight = 32x the verifier's actual concurrency of 4 and 4.3x the global semaphore of 30. Compose could multiply its verify concurrency 4x and its global cap 2x and still sit comfortably under the demonstrated-safe envelope; the first real ceiling it will meet is upstream latency/queueing (p95 tails), not 429.

recommended_settings.PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY: 24 (keep — writer was never the 429 source and raising it past the global semaphore is pointless until that is lifted)
recommended_settings.PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM: 64 (up from 30 — 30 currently clamps the 24x2=48 writer fan-out plus judge slots; 64 removes the clamp while peak burst stays ~10-12 req/s, under the 17.6 req/s demonstrated-safe floor)
recommended_settings.PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY: 16 (up from unset/4 — this is the highest-leverage change: verify serialization at 4 is what blows pre-pass batches past wall_cap. Probe was clean at 32/64/128, but judge_concurrency.py's own comment records a real friendli 429-storm at ~32 simultaneous, so 16 = half that documented storm threshold)
recommended_settings.PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S: 5 (down from 15 — every historical 429 recovered on attempt 1 and OpenRouter never sent Retry-After; the 15s floor is our own invention and overpays 3x per sporadic 429. Server Retry-After, if ever sent, is still honored verbatim by entailment_judge.py:503)
recommended_settings.PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_RETRIES: 5 (keep — the budget has never been stressed past attempt 1)

input.time_to_render_346: ~12.5 hours at CURRENT settings — this is the only number resting on a real measurement: the live post-fix iter4 run drafted 179 baskets in 6.44h (16 'pre-pass complete' batches, 08:33->14:59) = 27.8 baskets/hr, and 346/27.8 = 12.4h. At the RECOMMENDED settings, plausibly 7-10 hours: raising judge concurrency 4->16 attacks exactly the batches that measured worst (2567s and 2013s elapsed, abandoned=13 and 3, i.e. verify-serialization pushing past the 1700s wall_cap) — but that improvement is an extrapolation, because the fraction of wall-clock spent verify-bound was not separately measured. Assumptions made explicit: (1) the 27.8/hr pace includes all pipeline stages, same host, same corpus mix; (2) my probe contended with ~5 min of that 6.44h window (negligible); (3) upstream z-ai/glm-5.2 provider latency stays in its measured band (p50 ~1.5s judge, 6-12s writer); (4) no per-basket cost cliff — the live run spends ~$0.003-0.005/call and ~221 credits remain, ample for 346 baskets.
caveats: Probe was contended: pid 966951 (root, live iter4 compose) ran throughout, so 17.62 req/s and the p95 latencies are pessimistic lower bounds on account capacity, not clean maxima. The zero-429 finding is strengthened, not weakened, by this. A clean ceiling measurement requires killing/pausing PID 966951 and re-running /tmp/or_probe.py — only do that if the exact uncontended number matters.

The live process env block was unreadable (root-owned, /proc/966951/environ denied). Concurrency values were taken from its provably-parent launcher /home/polaris/wt/compose/launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh (lines 29-30, 37), corroborated by the pid cmdline matching that script's OUT path — but not read directly from the process.

'No rate limit found' is bounded by what was tested: no 429 up to 128 concurrent / 17.6 req/s sustained / 1184 requests. OpenRouter publishes nothing (no headers, /key requests=-1 deprecated), so a limit above that envelope cannot be excluded — it just cannot bind at anything compose is configured to generate, including the recommended settings.

The 17 historical 429s are real but all NLI-judge-side, all attempt-1 recoveries, likely per-provider (friendli et al.) transient noise rather than account-level limiting; the judge_concurrency.py comment about a friendli 429-storm at ~32 simultaneous is the reason the recommendation stops at 16, not the probe-clean 32+.

The 12.5h estimate assumes basket difficulty in the remaining set matches the measured 6.44h window; the observed per-batch variance is large (15.8s to 2567s per batch), so per-batch tail risk is real even though the aggregate pace was stable.

PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 (launcher line 37) was held constant in the recommendations; raising it multiplies writer fan-out and would need PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM re-derived (writer_conc x sections + judge slots).
